geo_gloss.sample: Ocean, sea, from Persian/Turkish دريا (Derya/Darya, the sea, the ocean). The sea in Islamic tradition is one of the most powerful symbols of divine abundance: 'And if all the trees on earth were pens

short_dict.sample: She who builds and makes flourish, feminine of Amir, from the Arabic root ʿ-m-r ('to populate, to make prosper'). The same root from which ʿumra ('lesser pilgrimage') is taken.
Servant of the Granter of Security, the first Almohad caliph who united North Africa and al-Andalus.
Servant of Al- Karim (the Most Generous). Maghrebi/French spelling of ʿAbd al- Karim . Al-Karim is one of the divine names of Allah.
Servant of Ar-Rahman (the Most Merciful). Maghrebi/French spelling of ʿAbd al-Rahman. The Prophet ﷺ said the names most beloved to Allah are ʿAbdullah and ʿAbd al-Rahman.
Servant of Ar-Rahman (the Most Merciful). Somali/East African spelling of ʿAbd al-Rahman.
Maghrebi/French spelling of Abu Bakr. The companion Abu Bakr al- Siddiq (RA) was the Prophet ﷺ's closest friend and the first Caliph of Islam.
Senegalese/Guinean Wolof-influenced spelling of Abu Bakr. Refers to Abu Bakr al- Siddiq (RA), first Caliph of Islam.
South Asian/Gulf spelling of Abu Bakr al- Siddiq (RA), the Prophet ﷺ's closest companion and the first Caliph of Islam.
The supporter, the fourteenth and last Fatimid caliph of Egypt, deposed by Saladin in 1171.
Moon-lord. Qutb al-Dīn Aibak, founder of the Mamluk dynasty of Delhi and the Qutub Minar.
Living, full of life. Malay/Indonesian spelling of Aisha . From the Arabic root ʿ-y-sh ('to live'). Refers to ʿA'isha bint Abi Bakr, wife of the Prophet ﷺ.
The just, regnal title of Sayf al-Dīn al-ʿĀdil, brother of Saladin and second Ayyubid sultan of Egypt and Syria.
From Fārāb, the city on the Syr Darya in Khurasan where he was born. From فارَاب (Fārāb), a Persian-Arabic toponym; the suffix -ī marks a person from the place.
The complete, the Ayyubid sultan who signed the 1229 treaty with Frederick II returning Jerusalem peacefully.
From Masʿūd, fortunate, blessed; the lineage marker of a happy line. From س-ع-د (s-ʿ-d), to be happy, fortunate; same root as مَسعود (Masʿūd) and the female سَعيدَة (Saʿīda).
From Yaʿqūb. Jacob, the biblical and Quranic patriarch. From the name يَعقوب (Yaʿqūb), the prophet Jacob, named in the Quran (2:132); the family lineage marker.
Sublime, elevated, variant of ʿAliyya, from the Arabic root ʿ-l-w ('to be high'). Al-ʿAli is one of the divine names.
Heroic lion, second sultan of the Great Seljuk Empire, victor at the Battle of Manzikert (1071).
The Helper, a regnal name carried by the thirty-fourth Abbasid caliph and by the fourth Almohad caliph.
Radiant lights. Guinean and Mauritanian variant spelling. Captures West African Francophone searches distinct from both Anouar (Algerian/Moroccan) and Anwar (Gulf/Pakistani).
Most exalted, highest in rank, from the Arabic root r-f-ʿ ('to raise, to elevate'). Feminine intensive of Rafiʿ.
Most noble, most honourable, from root sh-r-f (sharafa, to be noble). Ashraf is the superlative, not merely noble but the most noble. Top Egyptian and Arab boys name. Ashraf Ghani.
Askia the Great of Songhai, the imam-king who centralised Islamic administration across the Niger bend.
A Turkic warrior-name, the founder of the autonomous Khwarezmshah line under Seljuk overlordship.
Ornament of the throne, the sixth Mughal emperor (regnal title ʿAlamgir I), under whom the empire reached its greatest territorial extent.
Living, prosperous. South Asian/UK diaspora spelling of Aisha, from the Arabic root ʿ-y-sh. Refers to ʿA'isha bint Abi Bakr, wife of the Prophet ﷺ.
Living. Levantine/scholarly transliteration of Aisha (with -ah ending), from the Arabic root ʿ-y-sh.
Tiger, the founder of the Mughal Empire in India, descended from Timur on his father's side and from Genghis Khan on his mother's.
Splendour, joy, radiance, from the Arabic root b-h-j ('to be bright, joyful'). The visible beauty that gives pleasure to the eye.
The noble-generous. Bahlul Lodi was the Afghan founder of the Lodi dynasty of Delhi.
Bahman the king, regnal name of ʿAlāʾ ad-Dīn Bahman Shāh, founder of the Bahmani Sultanate of the Deccan.
Great leopard, the fourth Mamluk sultan of Egypt, victor over the Mongols and the Crusaders.
A young, tender tree, also: stature, build. From Arabic بانة, evoking a graceful, willowy figure.
Rain. Persian/Turkish باران, the blessed rain that the Quran repeatedly describes as a sign of divine mercy reviving dead earth.
Peace. Turkish baris (peace). Top Turkish boys name. Baris Manco, beloved Turkish musician. The quality of peace in the Turkish Islamic tradition.
Founder of the Circassian Burji Mamluk line of Egypt and Syria.
Leopard-lord. Burji Mamluk sultan who conquered Cyprus in 1426 and centralised Red Sea trade through Egypt.
Spring, from the Persian/Urdu بسنت, the season of yellow mustard blossom celebrated across South Asia.
A smile, from the Arabic root b-s-m ('to smile'). The Prophet ﷺ said: 'Your smile in the face of your brother is charity.'
Smiling. Egyptian and Levantine spelling variant. Bassem Youssef. Egyptian satirist. Same quality as Bassam but captures specifically Egyptian and Levantine searches.
The chaste one, the virgin devoted entirely to worship. Maghrebi spelling of Batool, an epithet of Maryam (mother of Prophet ʿIsa) and of Fatima al-Zahra.
Father of increase, borne by the great Sufi master Bāyazīd Bistāmī and by two Ottoman sultans, including Bayezid I, the Thunderbolt.
Blessing. Ethiopian Muslim boys name from Arabic barakaessing). 'And We blessed him and Ishaq' (37:113). Top Ethiopian and East African boys name.
Light/Proof. Ethiopian Muslim girls name from Arabic burhan (proof, light). Top Ethiopian girls name. Same divine-proof quality.
The last sultan of Granada. Muḥammad XII, who surrendered the city to the Catholic Monarchs in 1492.
Good news. Turkish form of Bushra. 'For them are good tidings (bushra) in the life of this world' (10:64). Top Turkish girls name. Busra Pekin. Turkish actress.
Sha'ban, month before Ramadan. Boys named after this blessed month. 'It is a month neglected by people between Rajab and Ramadan'. Prophet praised.
Royal falcon, standard Algerian girls form of Shaheen. Chahinez is how Algerian and French Muslim girls spell this name. Different from the Algerian variant Chahineze. Top Algerian
Thankful. North African boys namea, to be grateful, to thank abundantly). Top Algerian boys name.
Unique. Kabyle Berber girls name with no direct Arabic equivalent. Chanez represents pure Amazigh cultural naming within the Muslim tradition. Top Kabyle girls name.
Coast, shore, from Arabic shatia (shore, coast)es the girl of coastal quality, the liminal space where land meets sea. Top Kabyle girls name.
Prophet Shuaib. Moroccan variant spelling. Captures specifically Moroccan searches for this Prophet name. Same prophet of economic justice (26:181-183).
Grateful. North African/French spelling of Shukri. 'And Allah will reward those who are grateful (al-shakireen)' (3:144). Mohamed Choukri. Moroccan author of For Bread Alone. Top M
Guide, proof, feminine of Dalilo guide, to show the way). Dalila names the girl who guides, whose character shows others the right path. Top North African girls name.
God is my judge, from Hebrew. Danial ibn Luqman is mentioned in Islamic tradition as a prophet. Muslim converts keep Daniel, connecting it to the Prophet Danial. Top Western Muslim
Beautiful. Algerian -a ending variant of Djamila. Captures a distinct Algerian spelling pattern. Same quality as Djamila but with the -a ending used in Kabyle and specific Algerian
Of the gathering, the communal one. Kabyle/Algerian form derived from Arabic jamʿ ('gathering, community').
The world, worldly life. Maghrebi/French spelling of Arabic dunyā (دنيا), the present world as opposed to the hereafter.
Righteous ones. Turkish girls name. Same plural of barr quality. 'The righteous will be in delight' (83:22). Top Turkish girls name.
Moon-light. Turkish compound elan (moon) + nur (light). Top Turkish girls name. The double quality of moonlight: the moon's silver + the divine light.
Grace of the land, from Persian el (land, people) + naz (grace, coyness). Top Iranian female name for the past 30 years. Carries the quality of natural, easy, belonging grace.
The Helpers. Turkish form of Arabic Ansar (أنصار), the Medinan companions who supported the Prophet ﷺ after the Hijra.
Manly, vigorous, from Turkish er (man) + dal (branch). Erdal names the man who is himself the strong branch of a good tree. Top Turkish boys name.
Virtue, ethical quality, from Turkish erdem (virtue, moral goodness). Erdem names the man of virtue, not merely rule-following but genuine ethical quality. Top Turkish boys name.
A flower of Paradise, alternate spelling of Eshaal. A modern South Asian Urdu girls' name.
Name-light. Turkish compound esma (names) + nur (light). The names that are light. 'And He taught Adam all the names' (2:31). Top Turkish girls name.
Bond, safeguard, from root ain-s-m (isam, bond, safeguard). Essam names the man of safeguarding quality. Top Egyptian and Arab boys name.
Universe, cosmos, from Turkish evren (universe, the cosmos). Evren names the man of universal scope, whose quality encompasses rather than excludes. Top Turkish boys name.
Virtuous, of high merit, feminine of Fadil, from the Arabic root f-d-l ('to surpass, to be excellent').
Intelligent, quick-understanding to understand quickly). Fahima names the girl whose comprehension is swift and deep. Top Arab and Pakistani girls name.
Dawn. Indonesian spelling of Fajr. Surah Al-Fajr (Chapter 89): 'By the dawn.' Top Indonesian Muslim male name. The blessed Fajr prayer, the first prayer of the day, witnessed by an
Victorious. West African spelling of Fawziyya. 'He who purifies himself prospers (qad aflaha)' (87:14). Faouzia is top 5 in Senegal and Guinea.
Victory of ʿAlī, the king, second Qajar shah of Iran, under whom Persia fought the Russo-Persian wars.
Fatimah. Moroccan contracted form Fatna. Top Moroccan girls name. Same Prophet daughter quality. Captures Moroccan-specific Fatna searches.
Charming, fascinating, to captivate). Fatoon names the girl of captivating quality, whose charm is natural, not constructed. Top Arab girls name.
Decisive. French and Senegalese spelling of Faisal. Captures specifically Francophone West African (Senegal, Mali, Guinea) and French diaspora searches.
The victor, the successful one. Egyptian/Levantine spelling of Faiz, from Arabic root f-w-z ('to triumph, to attain').
Fragrant, wide and aromatic, an epithet of Damascus (الفيحاء), describing a place that breathes out perfume. From root f-y-h.
Jasmine flower, the Kabyle/Algerian word for jasmine, used widely as a girls' name in North Africa.
Knight, horseman. Levantine Arabic form of Faris (knight, horseman). The fearless mounted warrior. Top Levantine modern male name in Lebanon, Syria, Jordan. Distinct from any Faris
From the Persian/Arabic Faryāl, meaning ornament, jewelled headdress, common in Tunisia and the Maghreb.
Self-sacrifice, from root f-d-y (fada, to ransom, to sacrifice). Fida names the girl of devoted sacrifice quality, one who gives herself completely for what she loves. Top Arab gir
Victorious king. Firuz Shah Tughlaq, the long-reigning Tughlaq sultan of Delhi, builder of Firuzabad.
Noble flag, banner, from Persian/Arabic firyaloble flag). Firyal names the girl of banner quality, who stands high, visible, a mark of nobility. Top Levantine and Jordanian girls n
Radiant, shining, from Persian forouzan (ablaze, radiant). Forouzan names the girl who radiates, not quietly but actively, like a flame. Top Iranian girls name.
Victorious. North African spelling of Fawzia. Fouzia Assouli. Moroccan feminist. 'Whoever obeys Allah and His Messenger has achieved a great success (fawzan aziman)' (33:71). Top M
Rain, especially the first rain that revives the land, from Arabic غيث, used in the Quran for the rain that gives life.
Majestic, great. Egyptian spelling of Jalil. Al-Jalil ('the Majestic') is one of the divine names of Allah.
Beauty, grace. Egyptian spelling of Jamal, from the Arabic root j-m-l ('to be beautiful').
Precious, dear, expensive, from root gh-l-w (ghala, to be precious, costly, dear). The Gulf girl name of true preciousness. Similar meaning to Ghalia but shorter. Top UAE and Saudi
Precious, dear, costly, from Arabic غالي, that which is valued and not easily attainable.
Victorious, the one who gains spoils, from the Arabic root gh-n-m ('to gain, to win'). Levantine/Gulf form of Ghanim.
The Ilkhanid sovereign of Iran who embraced Islam in 1295 state religion of the Mongol Ilkhanate.
The warrior, the one who undertakes expeditions, from the Arabic root gh-z-w ('to set out on campaign'). Borne by the companion Ghazwan ibn al-Harith.
Wealth, sufficiency, from the Arabic root gh-n-y. Al-Ghani ('the Self-Sufficient') is one of the divine names.
Song, the act of singing, from root gh-n-y (ghanna, to sing). Ghinwa names the girl who is herself a song, whose life carries melody and joy. Top Levantine girls name.
Divine forgiveness. Tunisian spelling with -e ending. Same quality as Ghofran (Iranian/North African). Captures specifically Tunisian searches. Same divine forgiveness quality from
Tree branches, from root gh-s-n (ghusn, branch). Ghosoun names the girl of branching quality, each part extending from a strong root, each reaching further. Top Levantine girls nam
The cooing of doves, from Arabic هديل, the soft sound a pigeon makes for its mate, evoking gentleness and longing.
Hausa/West African spelling of Khadijah (with H- initial). Refers to Khadijah bint Khuwaylid (RA), the Prophet ﷺ's first wife and the first person to accept Islam.
Cooing. Egyptian/Levantine variant of Hadeel. The sound of the dove.
Praise, the act of praising. Gulf form of Hamd, from the Arabic root ḥ-m-d, the same root as Muhammad and Ahmad.
Praise, feminine of Hamd, from the Arabic root ḥ-m-d ('to praise').
Prophet Aaron. West African/Senegalese spelling. Senegal, Guinea, Mali, Niger. 'We gave him his brother Harun as a prophet' (19:53). Captures West African searches distinct from Ha
Aaron. (AS). 'My brother Harun is more eloquent than me in speech' (28:34). Top Nigerian and West African Muslim name. Distinct from Haroon page.
The beautiful one. Moroccan -ae ending variant of Hasnaa. Captures specifically Moroccan searches distinct from Hasnaa (standard). Same superlative beauty quality.
The beautiful, the good. Tunisian spelling of Hassan, from the Arabic root ḥ-s-n. Refers to Al-Hasan ibn Ali (RA), the Prophet ﷺ's grandson.
Resolute, decisive, firm in judgement, from the Arabic root ḥ-z-m ('to bind firmly, to be resolved').
Champion, great man, from Javanese/Indonesian hendra (great man, champion, from Sanskrit indra). Top Javanese Muslim male name. Used widely across Java (130M Muslims).
Generous, noble. North African/French spelling of Hisham. Hicham El Guerrouj. Moroccan Olympic gold medalist, world record holder in the mile.
Sharp sword. Egyptian/Levantine spelling of Hussam, the cutting blade. From Arabic husām.
Houris. Iranian and North African French spelling. Same paradise quality as Hoor but captures Iranian + French searches. Directly Quranic (44:54).
Scholarly Arabic spelling of Huzaifa. Hudhayfa ibn al-Yaman (RA) was a senior companion to whom the Prophet ﷺ entrusted the names of the hypocrites, known as Ṣāḥib al-Sirr (Keeper
The little beautiful one, scholarly Arabic transliteration with a single 'n' of Hussain. From the Arabic root ḥ-s-n. Refers to Al-Husain ibn Ali (RA), grandson of the Prophet ﷺ.
Lion ʿAli, invoking the title of Imam ʿAli ibn Abi Talib; the de facto founder of the Sultanate of Mysore.
Son of al-Bayṭār, the veterinary surgeon, the farrier. From بَيطَر (bayṭar), to practise veterinary surgery; the family trade became the family name.
Son of Khaldun, from khulūd, eternity, perpetuity. From خ-ل-د (kh-l-d), to remain, to be perpetual; same root as خُلد (khuld, paradise's eternity), خالد (Khālid, eternal).
Son of Rushd, rectitude, sound judgement, the right way. From رُشد (rushd), rectitude, mature judgement; same root as رَاشِد (rāshid, rightly-guided), found in the Quran (2:186).
Son of Ṭufayl, small child, the diminutive of ṭifl. From ط-ف-ل (ṭ-f-l), childhood, the early stage; from which طِفل (ṭifl, child) and طُفَيل (Ṭufayl, little one).
Son of Zuhr, light, brightness, the flowering form. From ز-ه-ر (z-h-r), to shine, to bloom; same root as زُهرَة (zuhra, flower; Venus). The female name Zuhra shares this root.
Father of nations. Western diaspora spelling (double-e) of Ibrahim. Refers to the Prophet Ibrahim (AS), Khalil Allah ('the Friend of God').
Ibrahim, the Fula/Mande West African form with final -a. Used across Guinea, Senegal, Mali, Gambia. Same Friend of Allah, same Prophet. Ibrahima is one of the top 5 boys names in W
Joy, delight, from root b-h-j (baha, to be joyful). Ibtihaj names the girl of overflowing joy. Ibtihaj Muhammad. American Olympic fencer. Top Arab girls name.
Excellence of Allah, compound boys name from ihsannce) + Allah. 'Allah commands justice and excellence (ihsan)' (16:90). Top Afghan and Pakistani boys name.
Prophet Ilyas. Greek/Balkan/Western spelling. Used by Greek Muslims, Bosnian/Albanian diaspora in Greece, and Western converts. Same Prophet (37:123).
Holder of the realm, the third sultan of the Mamluk dynasty of Delhi, true consolidator of the Delhi Sultanate.
Prophet Ilyas. Kabyle/Algerian Berber spelling. The Kabyle community (7M in Algeria) uses Ilyes as their standard. 'Indeed Ilyas was among the messengers' (37:123).
Faith. Moroccan/French -e ending spelling of Iman. Top 5 in Morocco. Same Quranic quality: 'And the believers, increase them in faith' (9:124). Captures specifically Moroccan searc
Family of Imran. French/Moroccan spelling. 'Allah chose the family of Imran above the worlds' (3:33). Imrane Koné. Franco-Malian footballer.
Sociability, friendlinessociable). Inas names the girl whose presence makes others feel at ease. Top Arab girls name.
Justicerly). Insaf names the girl of justice quality, not the justice of courts but the justice of character. 'Allah commands justice (al-adl)' (16:90). Top Arab girls name.
Victory, triumph, from the Arabic root n-ṣ-r ('to help, to give victory'). The verbal noun of intaṣara.
Iram/Irem, the legendary garden of paradise. 'Iram of the pillars, the like of which was not created in the land' (89:7). Turkish girls name from Quranic garden. Top 10 Turkish gir
Sunrise, radiant illumination, from the Arabic root sh-r-q ('to rise, to shine'). The light of the sun breaking the horizon.
Ismail of the Samanids, the amir who founded the great Persian Samanid state with Bukhara as its cultural capital.
Strong refuge, the place of safetyd, strength, strong fortress). Iyadh ibn Musa al-Yahsubi. Imam al-Qadi Iyad, the author of al-Shifa (In Praise of the Prophet). Top Arab boys name
Honour, dignity, to have honour). Al-Aziz (the Mighty) is a divine name. Izzat names the man of honour, whose dignity is natural and unforced. Top Arab boys name.
Majesty of the faith, borne by the last Khwarezmshah Jalāl ad-Dīn Mingburnu, by Rumi (Mawlānā Jalāl ad-Dīn), and by Mughal emperor Akbar (Jalāl ad-Dīn Muḥammad).
The one who draws, drags, or leads, from Arabic root j-r-r. Jarir ibn ʿAtiyya was a famous Umayyad-era Arab poet.
The jasmine flower. Western/English spelling of Yasmin, from the Persian yāsamīn (the fragrant white blossom).
Essence, jewel, gem, from Arabic جوهر (via Persian gawhar), denoting the inner substance of a thing as well as a precious stone.
World. Persian جهان (jahān), 'the world'. Used in Egyptian/Turkish Muslim communities.
Generosity, open-handed giving, from the Arabic root j-w-d. Al-Jawād ('the Most Generous') is one of the divine names.
Silver pearl. Levantine spelling of Jumana, from Arabic جمانة, a pearl of pure silver colour.
Companion Jubayr ibn Mut'im, he heard the Prophet reciting Surah al-Tur before his conversion and was so moved he accepted Islam. 'His heart flew out with love for the Quran.' Top
Damask rose, from Arabic جوري, a particularly fragrant variety of rose cultivated in the Levant and Persia.
The universe, all of creation, from kawn (existence, universe). The totality of all created existence. Pakistani top 10 girls name. 'In the creation of the heavens and earth are si
Firm. Qays ibn al-Mulawwah, the poet who became 'Majnun Layla.' His story is the model for Sufi poetry of divine love. Kais is the French/North African form. Top Tunisian boys name
Kulthum. Arab dominant spelling variant. Umm Kalthoum/Kulthoum, the legendary singer. Prophet daughter Kulthum bint Muhammad. Captures Arab searches for this spelling variant.
The generous sovereign. Karim Khan Zand, regent of Persia from Shiraz, remembered for justice and refusing the title of shah.
Star, planet, from Arabic kawkab. 'We adorned the lowest heaven with lamps' (41:12). Kawkab al-Sharq. Umm Kalthoum nickname (Star of the East). Top Iraqi and Levantine girls name.
Abundance. Maghrebi/French spelling of Kawthar. Surah 108 (Al-Kawthar) opens: 'We have given you al-Kawthar', also the name of a river in Paradise.
Being, entity, top Gulf boys name. 'Kun fa-yakun. Be and it is' (2:117). The man of complete being.
Khadijah, the West African form with the final -t. Used across Nigeria, Senegal, Niger, Chad, Cameroon. The first Muslim, the Prophet's most beloved wife. 'She believed in me when
Goodness. North African/French spelling. Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia. 'Whatever good you do, Allah knows it' (2:215). Captures North African searches distinct from Gulf/Pakistani Kha
Goodness of the religion. Khayr + al-Din. Kheireddine Pacha, 19th century Tunisian reformer. 'Whatever good you do, Allah knows it' (2:215). Top Tunisian + North African boys name.
Pure water, the radiance of pearls, from Arabic لُمار, used in the Gulf for shining brightness and clear water.
The trustworthy. North African/Maghrebi form of Al-Amin, an epithet of the Prophet ﷺ. From the Arabic root ʾ-m-n ('to be trustworthy').
Shining dark beauty. Moroccan -ae ending form of Lamia. Captures specifically Moroccan searches for this name. Same shining dark lips quality.
Al-Amin (the Trustworthy). West African form. Senegal, Guinea, Mali. Same Prophet title Al-Amin given before prophethood. Lamine Diallo. Guinean footballer.
Nights, plural of Layla (ليلة, night). Used for the rich, dark beauty of the night.
Night. Bosnian spelling of Layla. Top Bosnian girls name. Lejla Vujadinovic. Bosnian basketball player. Captures specifically Bosnian and ex-Yugoslav Muslim searches.
The pilgrim. Maghrebi form of Al-Hajj, used as an honorific for one who has performed the Hajj.
Soft, gentle, from Arabic lian (softness, gentleness). Modern short form used across Arab, Malaysian, Indonesian, and global Muslim communities. Al-Latif (the Subtle/Gentle) is a d
Shield, the protector, alternate Gulf spelling of Lu'ay. Lu'ay ibn Ghalib was a major ancestor of the Quraysh, and of the Prophet ﷺ.
Of gentleness, kindness. Tunisian/Maghrebi nisba form of Lutfi, from the Arabic root l-ṭ-f ('to be gentle'). Al-Latif is a divine name.
Storax tree. Arabic lubna (the storax tree, known for sweet fragrant gum). Top Moroccan, Algerian girls name. Loubna Abidar. Moroccan actress.
Pure silver. Maghrebi/French spelling of Lujain. Surah Al-Insan (76:15) describes vessels of silver passed among the dwellers of Paradise.
Companion, the friend. Kabyle/Berber name carrying the sense of intimate companionship.
Frankincense, the aromatic resin (Arabic لُبان) burnt as incense, prized in Arabia since antiquity.
Light-bearer, from Latin lux (light). Muslim converts in Brazil, France, Spain keep this name. Lucas bridges Islamic light (noor) concept with Western heritage.
Ways of ascent Surah Al-Maarij (Chapter 70). From Allah, Owner of the ways of ascent (dhi al-maarij) (70:3). The angels ascend to Him in a day of fifty thousand years (70:4).
Moon-phase, full moon quality, from Persian mah (moon) + am. Pakistani top 5 girls name. Maham Anga, regent of Emperor Akbar, most powerful woman in Mughal history.
Praised. Mahmoud is top 5 in multiple Arab countries and the Maqam Mahmud (praised station) in the Quran. Mahmoud Darwish. Palestinian poet.
Blessed, safe. Malay/Singapore standard form. Maymunah bint al-Harith (RA) was the last Mother of Believers to die. Top Singapore and Malaysian female name. Distinct from Pakistani
Queen. South Asian doubled-e spelling of Malika, feminine of Malik. From the Arabic root m-l-k ('to own, to rule'). Al-Malik is a divine name.
King of kings, the third sultan of the Great Seljuks, under whom the empire reached its zenith with Nizam al-Mulk as vizier.
Praised, from root m-d-h (madaha, to praise). The praised one, celebrated for genuine excellence. Same root as Ahmad and Muhammad. Top Muslim boy name in Egypt, Gulf, and Arab worl
The trusted, the one given safekeeping. Regnal name of the seventh Abbasid caliph who founded the formal Bayt al-Hikma in Baghdad.
King Mūsā, the tenth Mansa of Mali, famous for his 1324 ḥajj that reshaped global gold markets.
Wish, aspiration, that which is sought, from the Arabic root r-w-m ('to desire, to aim for').
Walking with grace and gentle sway, from root m-y-s (to walk with graceful ease). Maysa names the girl whose movement is naturally elegant. Top Saudi and UAE girls name.
Honey-sweetness, from Greek méli ('honey'). Used in some Turkish and Western convert communities.
Honey bee. Turkish form of Greek mélissa ('bee'), the honey-gatherer.
Doe, gazelle. Turkish word for the female deer (from Persian māral), evoking gentle grace.
Al-Marwa. Turkish form of Marwa. Top 10 Turkish girls name. 'Indeed Safa and Marwa are among the symbols of Allah' (2:158). Captures specifically Turkish searches.
Tender affection, delight, a Levantine girls' name, often associated with the Aramaic/Greek sense of 'sweet, gentle'.
The most praised, common English/diaspora spelling of Muhammad ﷺ. From the Arabic root ḥ-m-d ('to praise'). The most-used name on earth.
The doer of beautiful good, from the Arabic root ḥ-s-n. The muḥsin lives by iḥsān, defined by the Prophet ﷺ as 'to worship Allah as though you see Him'.
Chosen. Mokhtar is top 5 in Algeria, Mauritania, Mali. 'Allah chose (istafa) Adam and Noah and the family of Abraham' (3:33). Every mokhtar/mukhtar variant covered.
The praised one. West African (Senegalese/Mauritanian) spelling of Muhammad ﷺ. From the Arabic root ḥ-m-d ('to praise').
Muhammad, the Fula form with -adou suffix. Guinea and Senegal specific. Different from Mamadou (compressed) and Amadou (abbreviated). Captures this specific Fula sub-community.
The most praised. West African (Mauritanian/Senegalese) spelling of Muhammad ﷺ. From the Arabic root ḥ-m-d.
The believer. North African/French spelling of Mumin. Al-Mumin is a divine name (59:23). 'The believers are only those whose hearts tremble when Allah is mentioned' (8:2). Morocco,
The just, the impartial. Tunisian/Maghrebi form of Munsif, from the Arabic root n-ṣ-f ('to do justice, to be fair').
The desired, wished-for. North African spelling of Murad. Used across Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia. Mourad Bourboune. Algerian writer. Same quality: the man who is himself the answer
Maghrebi/West African spelling of Musa, the Prophet Musa (AS, Moses), the most-mentioned prophet in the Quran (136 times).
Refuge, alternate Pakistani spelling with uu-aa. Same Muadh ibn Jabal, the companion of supreme knowledge. Muaaz captures searches that use this doubled-vowel romanisation.

Ecstasy, the rapture of finding, from the Arabic root w-j-d, the same root from which Sufis derive wajd (mystical ecstasy).
Flash, glint, a brief flicker of light, from the Arabic root w-m-ḍ.
God's gracious gift, compact spelling of Waniya. A modern Urdu girls' name carrying the sense of a gift bestowed by divine grace.
Rose, 3-letter form)' (70:9), in beauty the rose is the standard. Ward names the girl of rose quality. Top Arab girls name.
Describer, the one of eloquent description, from the Arabic root w-ṣ-f ('to describe').
Means of approachach). 'Seek the means (al-wasila) of approach to Him' (5:35). Same as Ouassila.
Of long, full eyelashes. Arabic وطفاء, a classical descriptor for eyes shaded by thick lashes, considered beautiful.
The one of firm trust, the ninth Abbasid caliph, son of al-Muʿtaṣim.
Love, affection. Egyptian spelling of Widad. Al-Wadud ('the Most Loving') is one of the divine names of Allah.
Feeling, sentiment, conscience, from the Arabic root w-j-d ('to find, to feel deeply'). Plural of wijdan.
Union, reunion. Gulf spelling of Wisal, from the Arabic root w-ṣ-l ('to join, to reach'). In Sufi poetry, wiṣāl is the union with the Beloved.
Union, reunion, from the Arabic root w-ṣ-l ('to join, to reach'). In Sufi poetry, wiṣāl is the moment of union with the Beloved.
Union, reunion. Moroccan spelling of Wisal, from the Arabic root w-ṣ-l. Sufi term for spiritual union with the Beloved.

Maghrebi/West African spelling of Yaqub, the Prophet Yaʿqub (AS, Jacob), father of the twelve patriarchs and of Prophet Yusuf (AS).
Jacob. (AS). The patient father who waited decades for his son Yusuf. 'I only complain of my anguish and grief to Allah' (12:86). Top Nigerian and West African Muslim male name.
Certainty, sure knowledge, from the Arabic root y-q-n. The Quran speaks of three degrees: ʿilm al-yaqīn, ʿayn al-yaqīn, and ḥaqq al-yaqīn.
Jasmine, the French and North African -a ending form of Yasmine. Yasmina Khadra, pen name of Algerian author Mohammed Moulessehoul. Top French Muslim girls name.
Ease, the bringer of ease. English-spelling form of Yasir, from the Arabic root y-s-r. 'Allah desires ease for you and does not desire hardship' (2:185).
An old South Arabian name borne by Sayf ibn Dhi Yazan, a 6th-century Himyarite king of Yemen who expelled the Abyssinians.
He shall increase / one who augments, a verbal-name form. Historically borne by three Umayyad caliphs.
He shall live. Egyptian/Levantine form of Yahya, the prophet John the Baptist (AS). From the Arabic root ḥ-y-y ('to live').
Unique, the only one. Persian یکتا, used as a Turkish/Iranian girls' name.
Hebrew/Western form of Yusuf, the Prophet Yusuf (AS), whose story occupies the longest continuous narrative in the Quran (Surah Yusuf, 12).
Ease. Egyptian spelling of Yusra. Yosra. Egyptian actress. 'With hardship comes ease (yusran)' (94:5). Captures Egyptian searches distinct from classical Yusra.
Prophet Yusuf, the Fula/Peul West African form. Guinea, Senegal, Mali, Niger. Youssoupha. Congolese-French rapper. Same most beautiful story in the Quran (12:3).
Dove, also the name of the prophet Yunus (AS, Jonah), used as a girls' name in Turkey and the Maghreb.
Ease, gentle facility, feminine diminutive of Yusr, from the Arabic root y-s-r ('to be easy').
Ease, from the Arabic root y-s-r. The Quran promises in Surah Ash-Sharh (94:5–6): 'With every hardship there is ease.'
Yūsuf son of Tāshfīn, the Almoravid amīr al-muslimīn who unified al-Andalus and the Maghreb in the late 11th century.
South Asian Urdu spelling of Zahra, 'the radiant, the blossoming one'. From the Arabic root z-h-r ('to flower, to shine').
Victorious, the one who attains, feminine of Zafir, from the Arabic root ẓ-f-r ('to triumph, to obtain').
Blooming, radiante radiant). Zahiya names the girl of blooming radiance. Top Arab girls name.
Growing, increasing, from root z-y-d (zaada, to increase). Zaida names the girl of increasing quality, whose blessings grow, whose goodness multiplies. Top Arab girls name.
The Prophet Zakariyya (AS, Zechariah), father of the Prophet Yahya (John the Baptist). His prayer for a son in old age is recorded in Surah Maryam (19:2–6).
Pure, intelligentlligent). 'He has succeeded who purifies it' (91:9). Top Arab girls name.
Pure, untainted, feminine of Zakiy, from the Arabic root z-k-w ('to be pure, to grow in goodness'). The same root as zakāt.
From Persian zarrīn ('golden'), also the name of an ancient Hausa city in northern Nigeria, now used as a girls' name in West Africa.
Growth, abundant increase, variant of Zidan. From the Arabic root z-y-d ('to grow, to increase').
Adornment, beauty. Levantine spelling of Zayn. Zayn al-ʿAbidin (RA) was the great-grandson of the Prophet ﷺ, the only adult Hashemite male to survive Karbala.
Zainab. Tunisian spelling with -ei-. Zeineb is consistently top 3 in Tunisia. Captures specifically Tunisian searches distinct from Zineb (Moroccan) and Zeynep (Turkish).
Abundance, growth. French and North African spelling of Ziyad. Ziad Rahbani. Lebanese musician and playwright. Same quality of growth and abundance. Captures Francophone Muslim sea
Adornment, ornament. Persian/Urdu form of Arabic zīna. Surah Al-Aʿraf (7:31) speaks of taking one's zīna for the place of prayer.
Adornment of religion. Maghrebi/French spelling of Zayn al-Din, popularised by the footballer Zinedine Zidane.
Flowers, blossoms. Arabic plural of zahra ('flower'). Maghrebi spelling of Zuhour.
Bright, flourishing. Algerian/French spelling of Zuhayr. From the Arabic root z-h-r ('to bloom, to shine'). Zuhayr ibn Abi Sulma was one of the great pre-Islamic poets.
Nearness to Allah, shorter form of Zulfaa. 'We feed you only seeking zulfa (nearness) to Allah' (76:9). Captures 4-letter variant searches.
Nisba from Battan, a place near Harran in upper Mesopotamia. Place-name nisba from Battān in the region of Harran.
Nisba from Tūs in Khurasan, his birthplace. Place-name nisba from Ṭūs (Khurasan).
Nisba from Fergana, the valley in modern Uzbekistan. Place-name nisba from Farghāna (Central Asia).
From khāzin, treasurer or keeper, referring to his early position in the court treasury. From the Arabic khāzin (treasurer, keeper), occupational nisba.
'Son of Sīnā', a family name of Persian origin. Patronymic 'son of Sīnā', the family name; Latinised as Avicenna via the Hebrew Aven Sina.
Abū Bakr, a kunya meaning 'father of Bakr'; al-Rāzī, nisba from Rayy near present-day Tehran. Personal kunya plus place-nisba from Rayy. Latinised as Rhazes.
'Son of the Precious One', from al-nafīs, meaning precious, valuable. Patronymic from the Arabic root n-f-s, with al-nafīs meaning 'the precious'.
Ḥunayn, diminutive of ḥanīn, longing or affection, a Christian Arab name. Ibn Isḥāq, son of Isaac. Ḥunayn is a personal name from the root ḥ-n-n (yearning, affection); ibn Isḥāq is
